question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/config/types.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Response.MaxTransactionsInBlock] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that MaxTransactionsInBlock is an integer with no minimum value validation, and if it is set to zero or negative (misconfiguration), the /block endpoint would return zero transactions in the Transactions array and all transactions in OtherTransactions, making the block appear empty to any client that only reads the Transactions field?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/config/types.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NodeMap] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NodeMap entries are keyed by 'host:port' strings with no validation of the port range or IP address format, and if a malformed entry is present (e.g., a port of 0), the SDK may silently skip that node or fail to connect, causing transactions to be routed only to the remaining nodes and potentially changing the consensus timestamp distribution across blocks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/config/types.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: Db.StatementTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a very short Db.StatementTimeout (e.g., 1 second) to cause all /block requests for blocks with many transactions to time out, making those blocks appear to have no transactions, while blocks with few transactions succeed, creating a selective history reorganization where only high-volume blocks are affected?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/config/types.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: CommonConfig.Realm / CommonConfig.Shard] Can an unprivileged external user query the /account/balance endpoint with an account ID that uses a different shard/realm than CommonConfig.Shard/Realm, causing the entity ID encoding to produce a different EncodedId than the one stored in the database, resulting in the account's transaction history being attributed to a different entity and appearing reorganized relative to the actual ledger?
